Easy: What is a subject you once dreaded in school that you now appreciate, and what changed your perspective on it?
Who was the most memorable teacher you ever had, and what made their classroom feel different from all the others?
What is a lesson your parents or grandparents taught you at home that no school curriculum could have given you?
If you could go back and tell your younger self one thing about the value of education, what would you say?
What book, outside of required reading, has had the greatest impact on how you see yourself and the world?
Describe a moment when learning something new completely changed the way you thought about a problem.
What skill did you teach yourself out of pure passion, and how has it contributed to who you are today?
What is the most important life lesson you learned not in a classroom but through a personal experience?
How did your earliest memories of school shape the way you approach learning now?
What subject in school made you feel most alive, and why do you think it resonated so deeply with you?
If your education were a journey, what would you say was the most defining turn in the road?
What does it mean to you to be a lifelong learner, and how do you practice that in your everyday life?
Can you describe a time when failing at something taught you more than succeeding ever could?
What non-academic skill, such as cooking, gardening, or building, has taught you the most about patience and persistence?
How did a mentor outside of school help shape the direction your life has taken?
What is something you learned from a sibling, friend, or peer that formal education never covered?
How do you personally define intelligence, and do you think schools measure it accurately?
What childhood experience sparked your love of learning and continues to fuel your curiosity today?
If you could add one class to every high school curriculum, what would it be and why?
What is one thing about your own education that you are deeply grateful for, even if you did not appreciate it at the time?
How has travel, whether near or far, educated you in ways that a classroom simply could not?
What lesson from your upbringing continues to guide how you handle challenges and adversity?
What teacher or coach saw potential in you before you could see it in yourself, and how did that moment change you?
How did learning to read unlock a world for you that might otherwise have remained closed?
What is a question you asked as a child that still fascinates you and drives your desire to learn today?
What role has music, art, or another creative subject played in your overall education and emotional growth?
How did a difficult period in your school years ultimately make you a stronger and more resilient person?
What do you think is the single greatest gift a good education gives a young person?
How have your cultural traditions and family history served as an education you carry with you everywhere?
What is something you learned from a volunteer experience or community service that changed your outlook on life?
If your favorite teacher could see you now, what do you hope they would think about the person you have become?
What is the most creative way you have ever learned something, and why did that method stick with you?
How has making mistakes in a learning environment helped you grow in ways that getting everything right never could?
What is a personal passion or hobby that has taught you discipline, dedication, and the joy of mastery?
How do you stay intellectually curious in a world that often rewards quick answers over deep thinking?
What is a piece of advice a teacher gave you that you still carry with you in your daily life?
How did learning a second language, or being exposed to one, open new doors of understanding for you?
What do you think schools do exceptionally well, and what is one area where you believe they could do better?
How has reading biographies or the stories of remarkable individuals shaped your own ambitions and values?
What is something you learned from nature, animals, or the outdoors that a textbook could never fully capture?
How did your experience with extracurricular activities complement your academic education in meaningful ways?
What does the phrase 'learning by doing' mean to you, and can you share an example from your own life?
How has your education, both formal and informal, prepared you to be a more empathetic and compassionate person?
What is a subject or skill you wish you had been introduced to earlier in your education, and why?
How did a specific classroom project, assignment, or experiment ignite a passion that still burns in you today?
What is the most important conversation you have ever had that felt like an education in itself?
How do you balance absorbing information from others with forming your own independent thoughts and opinions?
What is one lesson from your elementary or middle school years that has stayed surprisingly relevant in your adult life?
How has overcoming a fear, whether of public speaking, math, or something else, shaped your confidence as a learner?
What is the best question a teacher ever asked you that made you stop, think deeply, and reconsider everything you knew?
How has the community you grew up in served as an informal classroom for the values you hold most dear?
What does it feel like when something finally clicks after you have struggled to understand it, and what does that experience teach you about perseverance?
If you could spend one day learning from any person in history, who would it be and what would you want to understand?
How has your faith, spirituality, or personal philosophy contributed to your understanding of the world around you?
What is a small, quiet moment of learning, one that happened unexpectedly, that turned out to be enormously significant in your life?
How did learning to lose gracefully teach you something that winning never could?
What is one academic experience that pushed you far outside your comfort zone and ultimately rewarded you for going there?
How has the experience of teaching or explaining something to someone else deepened your own understanding of it?
What role has self-discipline played in your education, and how did you develop it?
How do you think technology has personally changed the way you learn, for better or for worse?
What is the most interesting or surprising thing you have ever learned about your own culture or heritage?
How has watching your parents or guardians navigate their own lives taught you lessons that no school ever could?
What is a time when asking for help was one of the most educational decisions you ever made?
How did a class that seemed irrelevant at the time later reveal itself to be exactly what you needed?
What is a piece of wisdom passed down through generations in your family that you consider part of your education?
How do you think the friendships you formed in school contributed to your personal and intellectual development?
What is the most meaningful feedback you ever received from a teacher, and how did it help you grow?
How has being part of a team, whether in sports, arts, or academics, taught you something about collaboration and shared purpose?
What is a personal challenge you worked through that required you to become your own best teacher?
How has exposure to different perspectives, whether through people, books, or experiences, enriched your education?
What is a memory from school that still makes you smile, and what does it tell you about the power of a positive learning environment?
How do you think imagination and creativity contribute to education in ways that memorization and testing simply cannot?
What is the greatest academic compliment you have ever received, and how did it push you to aim higher?
How did learning about a historical figure or event reshape the way you understand your own place in the world?
What is something you learned from a grandparent or elder in your community that has become a guiding principle in your life?

What is one thing you believe every student deserves to experience in their education, regardless of where they grow up?
How did a field trip, outdoor experience, or hands-on project make a lesson more vivid and lasting than any lecture could?

How has your education given you a voice, and how do you use that voice to make a difference?
What is the most valuable thing you have learned about yourself through the process of formal education?
How do you think the lessons we learn in childhood continue to echo through every stage of our lives?
What is a time when a classmate, not a teacher, taught you something profoundly important?
How has learning to organize your thoughts and express them clearly changed the way you move through the world?
What is one educational experience that made you feel genuinely proud of yourself, and why did it matter so much?
How do you think kindness and emotional intelligence are as important as academic achievement in a well-rounded education?
What is a lesson about money, responsibility, or independence that you wish had been part of your formal schooling?
How has exposure to the arts, whether visual, performing, or literary, broadened your understanding of what education can be?
What is the most powerful story you have ever heard that taught you something you could not have learned any other way?
How did the structure and routine of school teach you something about the discipline you would need later in life?

How has the experience of being a student taught you what it means to be humble and always willing to grow?
What is a piece of advice about learning that you would give to a younger student just starting their educational journey?
How do you think a love of learning, once ignited, continues to shape a person's entire life and legacy?
What is the most unexpected place you have ever found a meaningful lesson, and what did it teach you?
How has growing up in your particular community shaped the lens through which you view education and opportunity?
What is something you believe every young person should know about the relationship between education and freedom?
How did a moment of genuine intellectual excitement, when an idea truly captivated you, remind you why education matters?
What is one habit of mind you developed through your education that you believe will serve you for the rest of your life?
How has learning about the achievements of people who looked like you, or came from where you came from, fueled your own aspirations?
What is a time when curiosity led you down a path of discovery that fundamentally changed something you believed?
How do you think the relationship between a student and their teacher can be one of the most transformative bonds in a person's life?
What is a lesson about character, courage, or integrity that you believe is more important than anything taught in a standardized curriculum?
How has your education, in all its forms, helped you become not just a more knowledgeable person but a better one?
What is one word you would use to describe what education has meant in your life, and why that word above all others?
How did working toward a difficult academic goal teach you that the process of striving can be as valuable as the achievement itself?
What is something you learned from observing someone you admired that changed the way you approached your own life and learning?
How has the experience of studying something you love with complete focus shown you what it means to be truly engaged?
What is a moment from your educational journey that made you realize you were capable of far more than you had imagined?
How do you think education, at its very best, has the power to help a young person discover who they truly are?
What is the most important question education has helped you learn how to ask, and why does asking it matter so much?

What is a time when your education gave you the tools to understand something happening in the world around you that would otherwise have felt overwhelming?

How has your education helped you navigate moments of uncertainty with more confidence and clarity than you might otherwise have had?

How do you think education shapes not just what we know but who we choose to become?
What is a time when learning something deeply personal about yourself through education changed how you relate to others?
How has the act of writing, journaling, or putting your thoughts into words served as a form of education in itself?

How do you believe the love your family showed for education influenced the value you place on it today?

How do you think the questions we are willing to ask say as much about our education as the answers we can give?

How has a moment of genuine wonder, when something in the world amazed you, reminded you why education is worth pursuing?

How did the experience of preparing for and performing in front of an audience teach you something about courage and communication?

What does educational equity mean to you in practice, and what concrete steps do you believe communities must take to achieve it?
How do you think the way a teacher chooses to present information can either ignite or extinguish a student's desire to learn?
What is the responsibility of a community when the schools within it are failing the children who attend them?
How do you think project-based learning, as opposed to traditional lecture and test models, changes the relationship students have with knowledge?
What role do you believe fathers, mothers, and family units play in shaping a child's educational identity before they ever enter a classroom?

What do you think is lost when schools are forced to cut arts programs, physical education, or extracurricular activities due to budget constraints?

What do you think is the most effective way to reach a student who has become disengaged from school and lost belief in their own potential?

What do you think happens to a community when a generation of young people grows up believing that education is not for people like them?

What is the most innovative teaching method you have ever encountered or heard of, and why do you think it works so effectively?

How do you think peer-to-peer learning and collaborative projects can develop skills that individual study simply cannot replicate?

What role do you think standardized testing should play in evaluating students, and where do you believe it falls short as a measure of true learning?

What do you think is lost when education becomes purely transactional, focused only on credentials and outcomes rather than genuine understanding?

What is the most meaningful reform you believe could be made to the way teachers are prepared, supported, and retained in the profession?
How can we ensure that the education children receive at home, through the values, stories, and practices of their families, is treated as a genuine asset rather than an obstacle?

What do you think is the most transformative thing that happens when a student who has never seen someone like them in a position of authority sees a teacher, principal, or professor who shares their identity?
How can we build educational systems that are nimble enough to adapt to a rapidly changing world while still providing the stability and consistency that students need?
What is the most important role libraries, both school and public, play in supporting the educational lives of children and families in a community?
How do you think schools can better teach students to care for the environment, understand ecological systems, and see themselves as stewards of the natural world?
What is the most effective way to involve parents who feel intimidated by, or disconnected from, their children's schools in a meaningful and empowering way?
How can we create school cultures where asking for help is seen as a sign of strength rather than weakness, and where no student suffers in silence?
What do you think is the most important way in which community members who are not parents can still contribute to the educational health and success of local schools?
How do you think the experience of being educated alongside peers from different cultural, economic, and linguistic backgrounds prepares students for a complex and interdependent world?
What is the most compelling thing you would say to a skeptical community member who does not understand why investing in schools benefits everyone, not just those with children enrolled?
How can we help students develop a sense of educational agency, the belief that they have the power to direct their own learning and shape their own future?
What do you think is the greatest missed opportunity in how most schools approach the teaching of social and emotional learning?
How can schools more intentionally build bridges between academic learning and the real-world challenges facing the communities in which students live?
What is the most important thing a school can do to ensure that students who arrive speaking a language other than English are welcomed, valued, and supported?
How do you think the experience of being placed in a lower academic track, with all the messages that sends about potential and worth, affects a student for life?
What is the role of imagination in education, and how can teachers cultivate it even within the constraints of a standardized curriculum?
How can we ensure that the teachers who work with our youngest and most vulnerable children are compensated, trained, and supported at the level their impact demands?
What do you think is the most powerful thing a school principal can do to create a culture of excellence, belonging, and joy in their building?
How can we encourage students who experience academic success to use that privilege to lift others rather than simply advancing their own interests?
What is the most effective way to break the school-to-prison pipeline and replace it with a school-to-opportunity pipeline for students who have been failed by the system?
How do you think the presence of strong, caring adults beyond parents and teachers, mentors, coaches, and community elders, fills critical gaps in a child's development?
What is the most important skill for navigating the modern world that most schools are not yet teaching with the seriousness and intentionality it deserves?
How can we ensure that schools in rural communities, which face unique challenges of isolation and resource scarcity, are not left behind in national conversations about educational improvement?
What do you think is the relationship between the health and well-being of teachers and the academic and social-emotional outcomes of their students?
How can we better support students who are the first in their family to pursue higher education, navigating systems that were not designed with them in mind?
What is the most meaningful thing a community can do to honor the teachers who work within it and signal that their labor is valued and their sacrifice is seen?
How do you think the way we talk about education in public discourse, as a product to be consumed rather than a right to be protected, has distorted our priorities as a society?
What is the most important thing you believe every child deserves to experience in their education, from the moment they enter school to the moment they leave it?
How can we ensure that the qualities we most want education to develop, curiosity, empathy, resilience, and integrity, are given as much attention as test scores and academic rankings?
What do you think is the responsibility of higher education institutions to examine and address the ways in which they have historically excluded and underserved certain communities?
How can we build a society that sees the education of every child, not just the wealthy and the fortunate, as a shared moral responsibility and a collective investment in our future?
What is the most urgent change you believe must happen in how we educate young people today if we want them to be prepared to lead and serve in the decades ahead?
How do you think the experience of coming of age in a connected, global world changes what education needs to provide and how it needs to be delivered?
What is the most powerful argument for teaching students not just content knowledge but the habits of mind, the ways of thinking and questioning, that allow them to learn anything?
How can we cultivate in every student the sense that they belong in the educational conversation, that their questions matter, and that their voice deserves to be heard?
What do you think is the most significant way in which the education system could be made more responsive to the actual needs and experiences of the students it is meant to serve?

How can communities create conditions where children arrive at school nourished, rested, safe, and ready to learn, rather than asking schools to compensate for what the community has failed to provide?
What is the most important thing you have learned about what makes a school truly great, beyond rankings, test scores, and college acceptance rates?
How do you think the very best educators manage to hold both high expectations and deep compassion for their students simultaneously, and why is that balance so rare and so essential?
What is the most meaningful reform to teacher compensation and career development that you believe could attract and retain the most talented and dedicated people in the profession?
How can we ensure that every student, no matter where they start, has access to the mentors, networks, and resources that will help them discover and pursue their full potential?
What do you think is the most undervalued form of intelligence that our educational systems consistently fail to recognize, nurture, and reward?
How can we create educational cultures that celebrate not just academic achievement but moral courage, civic engagement, and the willingness to stand up for what is right?
What is the most important conversation about education that communities across this country need to be having, but are not yet willing or ready to have?
How do you think the best schools manage to serve as both rigorous academic institutions and genuine communities of care, and what is required to make that possible?
What is the most effective way to teach young people to think independently and resist manipulation in a world where information is abundant but wisdom is rare?
How can we ensure that the education children receive reflects not just the knowledge of the past but the possibilities of the future they will be called to build?
What do you think is the most important thing communities owe to the teachers and schools within them, beyond just funding and resources?

How can we create a culture in which continuous learning, the willingness to grow and adapt at every stage of life, is considered not optional but essential to what it means to be a contributing citizen?
What is the most meaningful change you believe could be made to the physical design and structure of schools to better support the way students actually learn?
How do you think the act of reading together, in a shared community of readers, can build both literacy and the social bonds that support a thriving educational culture?
What is the most important thing schools should teach students about the relationship between individual success and responsibility to the community?
How can we create systems where the most experienced and effective teachers choose to work in the schools with the greatest need, rather than gravitating toward the most privileged settings?
What do you think is the most powerful way that schools can partner with families to create a seamless, consistent, and reinforcing educational experience for every child?
How can we address the very real crisis of teacher burnout and attrition without simply demanding more sacrifice from people who are already giving everything they have?
What is the most compelling vision you have for what a truly equitable, excellent, and joyful school could look like, and how do we get there from where we are?
How do you think placing students in positions of authentic leadership and responsibility within their schools transforms their sense of ownership and engagement with learning?
What is the most important thing you believe every educator should know about the students they serve before they can truly teach them?
How can we build educational systems that see students as whole people, with bodies, emotions, histories, and dreams, rather than simply as academic outcomes to be optimized?
What do you think is the greatest opportunity that technology offers for improving educational access and quality, and what is the greatest risk we must guard against?
How can we create pathways from education to meaningful work that do not require students to take on crushing debt or sacrifice their values in the pursuit of economic stability?
What is the most important thing we should be teaching the next generation about civic participation, democratic responsibility, and the power of an engaged and informed citizenry?
How do you think the experience of studying something that genuinely matters, that connects to real problems and real human needs, transforms a student's motivation and sense of purpose?
What is the most profound impact you believe a single teacher can have, not just on an individual student but on an entire community across generations?
How can we ensure that our educational institutions are not simply reproducing existing hierarchies and inequalities, but are actively working to dismantle them?
What do you think is the most essential quality a school community must cultivate in order for every student within it to feel genuinely capable and genuinely valued?
How can we create educational experiences that help students develop not just knowledge and skills but wisdom, character, and the capacity for ethical action?
What is the most important way in which the presence of strong, active, and supportive communities makes it possible for schools to do the work they were designed to do?
How do you think the practice of service learning, connecting academic study to real community problems and real community needs, transforms the meaning students find in their education?
What is the most urgent investment we must make in the educational infrastructure of our most underserved communities, and what is the cost of continuing to delay it?
How can we help every student understand that their education is not something being done to them but something they are actively building, and that they hold genuine power within it?
What do you think is the most important lesson a community can teach its children simply through the way it treats its most vulnerable and marginalized members?
How can we create educational systems that are designed from the start to see and serve every child, rather than systems designed for the average student that struggle to accommodate everyone else?
What is the most meaningful thing you believe a parent can do to support their child's education, even if they themselves did not have access to a strong formal education?
How do you think the best schools in the world, regardless of geography or resource level, share a common vision of what education is ultimately for?
What is the most important thing we must preserve about education as we move more and more of it into digital and virtual environments?
How can we build school communities where the act of caring for one another, of noticing when someone is struggling and reaching out to help, is woven into the educational culture itself?
What do you think is the most powerful thing that can happen when students are asked to apply what they have learned to a problem that is real, urgent, and meaningful in their own lives?
How can we ensure that the conversation about educational reform is led not just by politicians and policymakers but by the teachers, students, and families who live the reality of it every day?
What is the most important characteristic of a school that genuinely believes in the potential of every child who walks through its doors, and how does that belief manifest itself in daily practice?
How do you think we can better bridge the gap between what education promises and what too many students actually experience, and what is required to close that gap permanently?
What is the most transformative thing that happens when a student who has been told, explicitly or implicitly, that they are not capable, finally encounters someone who genuinely believes they are?
How can we ensure that the students who most need experienced, skilled, and dedicated teachers are the ones most likely to be taught by them?
What do you think is the most important element of a school's culture that cannot be measured by any assessment but is felt immediately by everyone who enters the building?
How can we make the decision to become a teacher not a sacrifice but a genuine calling that attracts the most talented, creative, and committed people in every generation?
Hard: How would you reimagine the entire architecture of formal schooling if you were freed from the constraints of tradition, politics, and institutional inertia?
What does a truly just education system look like, and what structural changes would be required to build it in a country still deeply divided by race and class?
How should nations address the global education crisis that leaves hundreds of millions of children without access to even basic schooling, and who bears the moral responsibility?
What is the relationship between an educated population and the health of democracy, and what happens to democratic institutions when large segments of the population are poorly served by education?
How do we build education systems that are genuinely responsive to the pace of technological change without sacrificing the deep, slow work of human formation that learning requires?
What would it mean to design an education system that centers the liberation and full humanity of every student rather than their productivity and economic utility?
How should artificial intelligence be integrated into formal education in ways that expand access, personalize learning, and preserve the irreplaceable role of the human teacher?
What is the systemic cost of an education system that consistently underinvests in early childhood development, and how do we make the political case for reversing that pattern?
How can wealthy nations discharge their responsibility to support educational infrastructure in the developing world without replicating the paternalism and cultural erasure of past interventions?
What does the increasing privatization of education mean for the democratic promise of universal access, and what is at stake when schooling becomes a market rather than a right?
How do we design learning environments that are genuinely trauma-informed, not just in name but in every dimension of their structure, staffing, and pedagogical approach?
What would it mean to fundamentally reimagine the role of the teacher, not as a transmitter of knowledge but as a guide, coach, and co-investigator alongside students?
How can education systems address the epistemic crisis of our time, the widespread erosion of trust in expertise, evidence, and shared truth, without sliding into indoctrination?
What are the ethical obligations of educational institutions that accumulate significant endowments and resources while the communities around them remain educationally underserved?
How should we think about the relationship between national education standards and local cultural autonomy in a diverse, multicultural society?
What does it mean to decolonize a curriculum, and what resistance does that project encounter, and why is it essential to undertake it despite that resistance?
How can education systems be redesigned to develop in students the emotional, relational, and ethical capacities that our current emphasis on cognitive achievement systematically neglects?
What is the relationship between teacher pay, teacher quality, and educational equity, and how do we make compensation reform politically viable at the scale required?
How should schools navigate the tension between preparing students for the economy as it exists today and educating citizens capable of reimagining the economy as it should be?
What are the long-term consequences of allowing the quality of a child's education to be determined primarily by the wealth of the zip code in which they happen to be born?
How do we build global educational frameworks that respect cultural difference and local knowledge while still working toward universal literacy, numeracy, and critical thinking?
What would a school designed around the emerging neuroscience of learning look like, and what sacred cows of traditional schooling would it require us to slaughter?

How should education systems respond to the reality that the jobs most students are being prepared for may not exist by the time they graduate, and new ones we cannot name are emerging in their place?
What is the moral case for free higher education, and how do we respond to the argument that universal access necessarily compromises quality and excellence?
How can education systems address the mental health crisis among young people without reducing schools to therapeutic environments and losing sight of their academic mission?
What does it mean to take Indigenous knowledge systems seriously as a source of educational content and pedagogical wisdom rather than merely as cultural artifacts?
How should we think about the purpose of higher education in an era when its economic returns are increasingly uncertain and its social stratification effects are increasingly visible?
What is the relationship between bilingual education, linguistic identity, and educational achievement, and why does the evidence in favor of multilingual learning so often fail to drive policy?
How can we design systems of teacher education and professional development that produce educators who are genuinely prepared for the complexity and demands of twenty-first century classrooms?
What are the unintended consequences of education technology platforms that collect massive amounts of data about children, and how should regulation keep pace with their proliferation?
How do we create educational pathways that honor the intelligence, creativity, and dignity of students who will not attend four-year colleges, without tracking them into a second-class future?

What is the relationship between educational attainment and civic engagement, and how do we design schools that produce active, informed, and morally courageous citizens rather than passive consumers of information?
How should schools engage with the profound questions of meaning, purpose, and values without either imposing a single worldview or abandoning the field to relativism?
What does transformative justice look like within an educational system that has historically relied on punitive discipline that disproportionately harms students of color?

How can education systems address the profound geography of opportunity, the fact that accident of birth location still largely determines the quality of schooling a child receives, both within nations and between them?
What is the responsibility of educational institutions to actively combat structural racism, not just in their admissions and hiring but in their curriculum, culture, and distribution of resources?
How do we build accountability systems in education that are genuinely oriented toward continuous improvement rather than punishment, shame, and the narrowing of curriculum?
What would it mean to genuinely center student well-being, rather than academic performance, as the primary measure of educational success, and what would schools have to change to get there?
How should we think about the tension between standardization, which allows for equity and comparability, and differentiation, which allows for the honoring of individual needs and identities?
What is the political economy of educational failure, the ways in which underinvestment in public schools is not accidental but serves the interests of those who benefit from inequality?

How can we create systems where the insights of cognitive science, developmental psychology, and neuroscience are genuinely integrated into classroom practice rather than remaining confined to academic journals?
What does genuine educational accountability look like when the outcomes we most care about, wisdom, character, civic courage, love of learning, are the hardest to measure?

How should education systems in the Global South navigate the tension between integrating into a globalized knowledge economy and preserving the indigenous epistemologies that are irreplaceable once lost?
What is the role of education in addressing the existential challenges of climate change, biodiversity loss, and ecological collapse, and are schools up to that responsibility?

How can we redesign the structure of schooling so that it produces graduates who are genuinely capable of collaborative, interdisciplinary problem-solving rather than solitary performance on isolated tasks?
What is the relationship between the concentration of educational privilege in elite institutions and the reproduction of social, economic, and political hierarchies across generations?

How should education systems address the profound disruption that generative artificial intelligence poses to the traditional markers of academic achievement, including writing, research, and problem-solving?

What does it mean to reimagine education as a lifelong entitlement rather than a front-loaded investment made in the first two decades of life, and what institutions would that vision require us to build?
How can we design educational systems that are genuinely capable of developing in students the kind of adaptive, creative, and ethical reasoning that no algorithm will ever be able to replicate?
What is the moral and political case for treating teachers as public intellectuals and professional leaders rather than as technicians implementing curricula designed by others?

How do we create educational systems that are simultaneously excellent for every student while refusing to use the fiction of meritocracy to legitimize outcomes that are actually the product of deep structural advantage?
What would it mean to take seriously the claim that play is the most powerful vehicle for human learning and development, and how would it transform the design of schools at every level?

How should education systems respond to the growing body of evidence that the standard school schedule, designed around an agricultural calendar and an industrial economy, is actively harmful to adolescent development?
What is the relationship between school segregation, whether by race, class, language, or religion, and the quality and character of the civic education students receive?
How do we create systems where the vast amounts of knowledge being generated about how children learn most effectively are translated into actual changes in classroom practice and school design?
What is the ethical framework we should use to evaluate education technology companies whose products are shaped as much by the imperatives of capital and growth as by the interests of learners?

How should we think about the tension between the universalizing aspirations of education, to give every child access to the full inheritance of human knowledge, and the localizing realities of community, culture, and place?
What would it mean to design an education system around the premise that every child is a genius in their own way, and what would we have to dismantle in order to build it?
How can education systems equip students to be not just consumers but creators of media, narrative, and knowledge in an era when the ability to shape information is itself a form of political power?
What is the relationship between educational access for girls and women and the broader developmental indicators of the communities and nations in which those girls and women live?

How do we build education systems that are capable of holding the tension between transmitting the best of what has been thought and discovered and challenging students to question, critique, and transcend that inheritance?
What is the most honest and rigorous thing we can say about the relationship between education, social mobility, and the persistence of inherited advantage in contemporary societies?

How should schools address the political and cultural polarization of the societies they serve without either reproducing division or pretending to a false neutrality that itself constitutes a political choice?
What does it mean to teach for wisdom rather than for mere competence, and what pedagogical, structural, and philosophical shifts would that reorientation require?
How can we build educational systems that see the cognitive and cultural diversity of their student populations not as a challenge to be managed but as a resource to be activated?

What is the relationship between housing policy, economic policy, and health policy on one hand and educational outcomes on the other, and why do education reformers so often refuse to engage with that relationship?
How should we think about the purpose of the humanities in an era when instrumental arguments about education, its role in job preparation and economic productivity, are increasingly dominant?
What would it mean to evaluate a school not by its test scores and college acceptance rates but by the quality of human beings it sends into the world, and how would we design such an evaluation?
How can education systems address the profound alienation many students feel from school, an alienation that research suggests begins as early as fourth grade and accelerates sharply in adolescence?
What is the relationship between student autonomy, the right to direct one's own learning, and the social responsibility of education to ensure certain common competencies and shared civic knowledge?

How should we think about the role of failure and struggle in learning in a culture that is simultaneously more achievement-oriented and more anxious about failure than any previous generation has been?
What does it mean to design educational systems that are genuinely anticipatory, that equip students not just for the present world but for the one they will need to help create?

How can we build global coalitions around educational investment that are powerful enough to overcome the political and economic forces that benefit from keeping populations uneducated and disempowered?

What is the most important reframe we need in how we talk about educational inequality, from a problem of individual deficiency to a problem of structural design, and what does that reframe demand of us?

How do we create educational systems that develop in students the capacity for genuine moral reasoning, the ability to think carefully and courageously about questions of right and wrong, rather than simply passing on received values?
What is the ethical case for eliminating student debt, and what does the persistence of that debt burden tell us about who our society truly believes deserves access to education?
How should schools navigate the increasingly blurred boundary between education and indoctrination in a political environment where every curricular choice is contested and every fact is disputed?
What would it mean to build schools that are genuinely responsive to the community context in which they are embedded, shaped by local knowledge, local history, and local aspiration?

How do we create educational systems that produce students who are not only skilled but genuinely oriented toward justice, toward using their education in service of a more humane and equitable world?
What is the relationship between school discipline practices, particularly those that criminalize ordinary adolescent behavior, and the broader phenomenon of mass incarceration?

How should we think about the long-term educational consequences of the pandemic, which revealed and accelerated inequalities that had been growing for decades, and what does genuine recovery require?
What is the role of education in cultivating the kind of deep cross-cultural understanding and genuine human solidarity that the challenges of the twenty-first century require?

How can we design higher education institutions that are genuinely committed to the public good rather than to the private advancement of their students and the financial interests of their investors?

What would it mean to build educational systems in which the knowledge and experience of students, particularly students from marginalized communities, are treated as genuine contributions to the curriculum rather than deficits to be overcome?
How should education systems address the profound ways in which social media and digital culture are reshaping not just what young people know but how they think, attend, and relate?

What is the most important thing that systems of education must do differently if they are to prepare students for a world shaped by artificial general intelligence, biotechnology, and climate disruption simultaneously?

How do we build educational institutions that are genuinely anti-racist, not in their rhetoric and mission statements alone, but in their curriculum, staffing, admissions, discipline, and distribution of resources?
What is the relationship between the feminization of the teaching profession and its relatively low pay and status, and what would genuine respect for teaching as a profession require us to change?
How should we think about the role of competitive academic rankings and sorting mechanisms in perpetuating inequality, limiting opportunity, and distorting the behavior of schools and families?

What would it mean to take the right to education seriously as an enforceable human right, not just a rhetorical commitment, and what international legal and institutional architecture would that require?
How can education systems develop in students the capacity to live well with uncertainty, ambiguity, and complexity rather than demanding the false clarity that is the refuge of the underprepared?
What is the relationship between the defunding of public education and the rise of educational alternatives that serve private interests while weakening the civic foundations of democratic society?
How should education systems address the intersection of poverty and academic underachievement in ways that are genuinely structural rather than merely compensatory or supplemental?
What would it mean to design a global education system that treated the preservation of linguistic and cultural diversity as a central goal rather than an obstacle to universal literacy?
How can we build educational cultures that are capable of holding both rigorous intellectual standards and genuine care for the full humanity of every person within them?

What is the most important thing we need to change about how we train, support, and evaluate teachers if we want teaching to become the most transformative profession in the world rather than one of the most neglected?

How do we create educational systems that are genuinely inclusive of students with disabilities, not just in physical access and legal compliance, but in the full recognition of their potential and their right to an ambitious education?

What is the relationship between digital connectivity and educational equity, and what would it mean to treat internet access and device ownership as educational infrastructure as fundamental as school buildings and textbooks?

How should we think about the limits of education as a solution to inequality, the ways in which schooling alone cannot compensate for the effects of poverty, racism, and structural disadvantage without being accompanied by broader social transformation?
What is the most important structural change we could make to how schools are funded that would meaningfully reduce the correlation between zip code and educational quality?

How can we build educational systems that are simultaneously accountable to high standards and genuinely responsive to the immense diversity of need, experience, and aspiration that every school community contains?

What does it mean to reimagine the relationship between education and work in a society where the traditional assumptions about career, credential, and economic security are all being rapidly dismantled?

How should schools address the profound ways in which colonialism, slavery, and other historical systems of domination have shaped both the content of curricula and the structure of educational institutions?
What is the most compelling vision of a post-scarcity educational future, one in which every human being has access to the full wealth of human knowledge and the conditions needed to pursue it?

How can we build systems of public education that are not only excellent and equitable but that generate the civic trust, shared identity, and common purpose that democratic societies require to survive and flourish?

What is the relationship between the arts, the sciences, and the humanities as modes of knowing, and why does the insistence on prioritizing some over others impoverish the minds of the students we are trying to educate?

How should education systems respond to the growing body of evidence that traditional homework practices, particularly for young children, produce more harm than benefit and deepen inequality rather than reducing it?
What would it mean to build educational systems designed from the start to serve the students who are hardest to serve, those who are most marginalized, most traumatized, and most underestimated?
How can we create the political will necessary to invest in education at the level that the complexity and scale of our shared challenges actually demand?
What is the most important ethical question that the deployment of artificial intelligence in educational settings raises, and how should schools, governments, and families respond to it?

How do we create educational systems that are not merely preparing students to succeed within existing structures but are actively developing the critical consciousness needed to transform those structures for the better?

What is the most important thing that education systems around the world can learn from one another, and what prevents that learning from happening at the scale and speed it must if we are to meet the challenges ahead?

How should we think about the profound responsibility of education to cultivate not just capable but genuinely good people, and what does that responsibility demand of every teacher, administrator, and policymaker?

What is the relationship between the imagination and the intellect in a fully realized education, and why does the marginalization of imaginative thinking in our schools represent not just an aesthetic failure but a civilizational risk?

How can we build education systems that are genuinely oriented toward the flourishing of every human being they touch, not as an aspiration but as a design principle, a standard against which every policy and practice is measured?

What does it mean to take seriously the idea that the measure of an education system is not the achievements of its highest-performing graduates but the quality of life and opportunity it makes possible for its most vulnerable students?

How do we create the conditions in which education can serve its highest purposes, the liberation of human potential, the cultivation of wisdom, and the building of a just and flourishing world, rather than merely serving as a sorting mechanism for an unequal economy?

What is the most urgent reimagining of schooling that the combination of climate crisis, technological disruption, democratic fragility, and profound human inequality demands of us, and do we have the courage and vision to undertake it?

How can we design education systems that honor both the universal dimensions of human learning and the irreplaceable particularity of local culture, language, and knowledge without sacrificing either to the demands of the other?

What is the most important thing that the history of educational reform teaches us about why even the most brilliant and well-resourced attempts at systemic change so often fail to produce lasting transformation?

How do we build educational institutions that are capable of teaching students to live with the radical uncertainty of the future while still providing the stability, structure, and sense of purpose that human beings need to learn and grow?

What would it mean for a society to truly put its money, its talent, and its political will where its rhetoric is and treat the education of every child as the most important collective project it has ever undertaken?

How should education systems address the profound crisis of meaning that affects so many young people today, the sense that the world they are being prepared for is not worth the sacrifice it demands?

What is the most important thing we could change about the way academic achievement is defined, recognized, and rewarded that would make our education systems more just, more humane, and more genuinely educative?

How can we ensure that the profound advances in understanding how humans learn are not captured exclusively by private technology companies and deployed in ways that deepen inequality rather than reducing it?

What is the relationship between the right to education and the right to cultural survival, and how do we build educational systems that expand opportunity without demanding the erasure of identity as the price of access?

How do we build a global movement for educational justice that has the moral clarity, the strategic sophistication, and the political power to overcome the entrenched interests that benefit from keeping education unequal?

What would it mean to take the evidence about what actually works in education, from neuroscience, developmental psychology, and decades of educational research, and make that evidence the foundation of policy rather than the exception to it?

How should we think about the relationship between the freedom to educate children according to the values of their family and community and the responsibility of the state to ensure that every child receives an education that expands rather than constrains their future choices?

What is the most important thing we can do today to ensure that the education systems we build for the twenty-first century serve not just the needs of the economy but the fullness of human potential and the demands of a just and sustainable world?

How can we reimagine schooling in ways that honor the complete and irreducible humanity of every student, that treat each child not as a future worker or a test score but as a bearer of dignity, potential, and the right to a rich and meaningful life?

What is the most fundamental question we have not yet been willing to ask about the purpose of education, and what would happen to our schools, our communities, and our civilization if we finally found the courage to ask it and to live by the answer?

How do we design educational systems that are not merely more equitable versions of what we already have, but genuinely transformative institutions capable of helping us build the kind of world that the challenges and possibilities of our moment demand?

What is the relationship between educational imagination and political courage, between the capacity to envision what schooling could be at its best and the willingness to disrupt the comfortable arrangements that keep it from getting there?

How can we create educational systems that are designed to produce not just graduates who can perform in the economy but human beings who are equipped, motivated, and determined to make the world more just, more beautiful, and more worthy of the children who will inherit it?
